one_line_takeaway: ALPHA is a CWE-hierarchy-aware penalty benchmark showing that open-source LLMs substantially outperform CodeQL and Semgrep at function-level Python CWE identification (mainly because the SAST tools fail to fire on over half the samples), but LLM prediction consistency across repeated runs varies enormously (8.26%-81.87% Perfect Agreement) and degrades sharply on datasets with hierarchically diverse ground-truth labels, which the authors argue makes consistency a first-class criterion for any LLM used as a feedback signal in iterative vulnerability-repair loops.
key_finding: LLMs substantially outperform both SAST tools under ALPHA on both datasets: best LLM ALPHA scores (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B: 574.7 on SVEN, 617.8 on SecurityEval; Devstral-24B: 695.1 on SVEN, 578.5 on SecurityEval) are far lower (better) than any SAST configuration (CodeQL Confidence: 5981.9 on SVEN, 1261.0 on SecurityEval; Semgrep Confidence: 3663.5 on SVEN, 1307.6 on SecurityEval), because CodeQL and Semgrep produced no prediction at all for over 50% of samples. When SAST tools do detect something, CodeQL is more precise (72.4% of its predictions contain the ground-truth CWE) than Semgrep (50.0%), and the near-identical Confidence vs. Any-Match ALPHA scores show SAST errors stem from missed detections, not mis-prioritised alternatives. Model rankings are stable across datasets and metrics (Spearman rho=0.86, p=0.014 for LLMs only; rho=0.92, p<0.001 including SAST; rho=0.96, p<0.001 for consistency rankings). DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B is the worst-performing and least consistent LLM (ALPHA 2557.0+/-194.3 on SVEN; Perfect Agreement as low as 8.26% on SecurityEval), while Qwen2.5-Coder-32B and Devstral-24B are consistently the strongest and most reliable. Perfect Agreement ranges from 81.87% (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B on SVEN) down to 8.26% (DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B on SecurityEval); the gap between Perfect and Majority Agreement averages 24.1 percentage points on SVEN and 36.1 points on SecurityEval. All models show markedly lower consistency on SecurityEval (more hierarchically diverse ground-truth labels: pillar/class/base/variant mixed) than on SVEN (uniform base-level labels only), e.g. Qwen2.5-Coder-7B drops from 71.64% to 21.49% Perfect Agreement between the two datasets.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ALPHA (Adaptive Learning via Penalty in Hierarchical Assessment), the first function-level Python vulnerability-detection benchmark that scores predictions at CWE-specific granularity using a taxonomy-aware penalty rather than binary vulnerable/not-vulnerable classification. ALPHA builds a directed graph G from the MITRE CWE hierarchy (pillar/class/base/variant/compound/chain nodes, excluding View and Category organisational nodes; 944 nodes, 1153 edges after exclusion vs. 993 nodes/1633 edges in the full graph) and defines a penalty P(cpred, ctrue) = d(cpred, ctrue) x alpha(cpred, ctrue), where d is the Dijkstra shortest-path distance on the undirected graph and alpha is a direction multiplier that differentiates three error types: over-generalising (ancestor prediction, fixed alpha_up=2.0), lateral (different branch, fixed alpha_lateral=1.8), and over-specifying (descendant prediction, adaptive alpha_down between 1.1 and 1.8 depending on how much 'room for specificity' exists below the true CWE, i.e. delta(ctrue)/Delta(type(ctrue))). Out-of-graph or disconnected predictions get a fixed penalty of doog=7 (ceil of the graph's mean pairwise shortest path, 6.26) times alpha_oog=2.5, i.e. P=17.5. The paper's own benchmarking exercise (its second contribution) evaluates seven open-source LLMs and two SAST tools (CodeQL v2.23.0, Semgrep v1.136.0) on two human-labelled, function-level Python vulnerability datasets (SecurityEval, 121 samples; SVEN-Python subset, 342 samples) under this metric, and separately measures each LLM's run-to-run prediction consistency (Perfect Agreement = identical CWE in all 3 runs; Majority Agreement = at least 2 of 3 agree) across three repeated runs per model at temperature=0.8 on an A100-40GB GPU.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender threat model in the classic security-paper sense; the paper is an evaluation/benchmark study. The implicit 'threat' addressed is LLM-generated code shipping exploitable vulnerabilities (the paper cites prior work claiming 40-60% of LLM-generated snippets contain flaws) and the practical question of which detection mechanism (SAST tool vs. LLM-as-detector) should supply the corrective feedback signal in an automated iterative code-repair loop. Ground-truth vulnerability labels in both evaluation datasets are human-annotated (not SAST-derived), which the authors deliberately require to avoid circularity in which SAST tools serve as both detector-under-test and label source.
limitations: Self-stated by the authors: (1) LLMs were constrained to a single CWE output per function for feedback-loop actionability, whereas SAST tools inherently produce multiple candidate findings; the authors argue this asymmetry does not materially favor SAST given the minimal Confidence-vs-Any-Match gap, but acknowledge the constraint departs from natural LLM behavior and that unconstrained multi-label LLM outputs showed substantially higher false-positive rates in preliminary checks. (2) Evaluation is limited to two Python datasets (SecurityEval, 121 samples; SVEN, 342 samples) at function-level granularity only; findings may not generalize to other languages or to repository/commit-level analysis. (3) Absolute ALPHA scores should not be compared directly across the two datasets due to differing sample sizes and difficulty/label-abstraction distributions, though rankings are stable. (4) The proposed dual-head fine-tuning architecture that would use ALPHA as a differentiable training loss (Section VI) is presented only theoretically -- it has no empirical validation in this paper; the authors explicitly flag convergence behavior, computational overhead, and downstream performance gains as unassessed. (5) All experiments used temperature=0.8 (non-deterministic decoding) to reflect realistic deployment, which the authors note is itself a source of the observed variance, conflating genuine model instability with sampling-induced variance to some degree. (6) The penalty weights (alpha_up=2.0, alpha_lateral=1.8, alpha_min=1.1) are fixed by construction to preserve a rank ordering, not empirically derived or validated against human judgments of diagnostic utility.

datasets_used: SecurityEval (Siddiq & Santos, 2022): 121 vulnerable Python functions; 73 base, 26 class, 16 variant, 3 pillar weaknesses
SVEN (He & Vechev, 2023): 342 Python samples filtered from the original 720 multi-language dataset; all labelled at base-weakness level
quantitative_results: ALPHA, SVEN / SecurityEval: CodeQL (Confidence) 5981.9 / 1261.0; CodeQL (Any Match) 5981.9 / 1212.9
ALPHA, SVEN / SecurityEval: Semgrep (Confidence) 3663.5 / 1307.6; Semgrep (Any Match) 3562.7 / 1307.6
CodeQL and Semgrep produced no prediction for over 50% of samples
CodeQL precision when it predicts: 72.4% of predictions contain the ground-truth CWE; Semgrep: 50.0%
Spearman rank correlation of ALPHA scores across datasets: rho=0.86 (p=0.014) for LLMs only; rho=0.92 (p<0.001) including SAST tools
Perfect Agreement (3/3 identical CWE) range: 81.87% (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B, SVEN) to 8.26% (DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B, SecurityEval)
Average gap between Perfect and Majority Agreement: 24.1 percentage points (SVEN), 36.1 points (SecurityEval)
Spearman rank correlation of consistency rankings across datasets: rho=0.96 (p<0.001)

CWE graph statistics: 944 nodes / 1153 edges after pruning View/Category nodes, vs 993 nodes / 1633 edges in full graph; mean pairwise shortest-path distance across graph = 6.26 (used to derive out-of-graph penalty distance of 7)
baselines_compared: CodeQL v2.23.0 (security-extended + security-experimental query suites, 101 built-in queries covering 123 CWEs)
Semgrep v1.136.0 (python, security-audit, owasp-top-ten, and bandit rulesets, 39 pattern-matching rules covering 17 CWEs)
Seven open-source LLMs: Qwen2.5-Coder-32B, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, Mistral-7B, Llama3.1-8B, Phi4-14B, DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B, Devstral-24B (all run via Ollama, temperature=0.8, 3 runs each)
